questions.question: What is REST and what are its core principles?
questions.answer:
REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for building networked services. It's not a protocol — it's a set of constraints that, when applied, make APIs predictable and scalable.

Core constraints:

  • Stateless — each request contains all information needed to process it. Server holds no client session state. This is why we use JWT tokens (client sends token on every request) instead of server-side sessions.
  • Client-Server — UI and data storage are separated. They evolve independently.
  • Cacheable — responses should declare themselves cacheable or not. GET requests should be idempotent and cacheable.
  • Uniform Interface — consistent URL conventions, HTTP verbs, status codes. Resources are identified by URIs. Representations (JSON/XML) are transferred, not the resource itself.
  • Layered System — client doesn't know if it's talking directly to the server or through proxies/load balancers.

In FolioTrack's ASP.NET Core 10 API, every controller action maps to a resource + verb. GET /api/portfolios/{id} returns a portfolio representation. The client never knows whether a cache or the actual controller handled it.
followUps: What makes an API RESTful vs REST-like?
What is HATEOAS?
REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC?
questions.tip: Statelessness is the most important constraint — and the most commonly violated. Server-side sessions break horizontal scaling. JWT moves state to the client, which is why it's the standard in .NET APIs.

questions.question: What is the difference between GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE HTTP verbs?
questions.answer:
HTTP verbs define the intended operation on a resource:

  • GET — retrieve a resource. Safe (read-only) + idempotent (same result every time). Response should be cacheable.
  • POST — create a new resource or trigger an action. Not idempotent — calling twice creates two resources.
  • PUT — full replacement of a resource. Idempotent — sending the same PUT twice has the same effect. Client provides the complete new representation.
  • PATCH — partial update. Only the fields provided are changed. More efficient than PUT for large objects. Not inherently idempotent (depends on implementation).
  • DELETE — remove a resource. Idempotent — deleting an already-deleted resource returns 404 but has no further effect.

In FolioTrack's API: POST /api/portfolios creates, GET /api/portfolios/{id} reads, PUT /api/portfolios/{id} fully updates, PATCH /api/portfolios/{id} updates just the name, DELETE /api/portfolios/{id} removes.
followUps: What does idempotent mean?
When would you use PATCH over PUT?
What is a safe method?
questions.tip: PATCH is underused but important for performance — updating a user's display name shouldn't require sending their entire profile. In .NET, use JsonPatchDocument<T> from Microsoft.AspNetCore.JsonPatch for RFC 6902 compliant PATCH.

questions.question: What are common HTTP status codes and what do they mean?
questions.answer:
Status codes tell the client what happened. They're grouped by first digit:

2xx Success:

  • 200 OK — standard success. GET, PUT responses.
  • 201 Created — resource was created. POST responses should return 201 + Location header pointing to the new resource.
  • 204 No Content — success but no body to return. DELETE and sometimes PUT.

3xx Redirection:

  • 301 Moved Permanently — resource moved; update bookmarks.
  • 302 Found — temporary redirect.

4xx Client Error:

  • 400 Bad Request — invalid input (validation failure, malformed JSON).
  • 401 Unauthorized — not authenticated (missing/invalid token).
  • 403 Forbidden — authenticated but not authorized for this resource.
  • 404 Not Found — resource doesn't exist.
  • 409 Conflict — state conflict (e.g., duplicate email).
  • 422 Unprocessable Entity — syntactically valid but semantically wrong.

5xx Server Error:

  • 500 Internal Server Error — unhandled exception.
  • 503 Service Unavailable — overloaded or down for maintenance.
followUps: What's the difference between 401 and 403?
When do you use 422 vs 400?
questions.tip: 401 vs 403: 401 = "I don't know who you are" (unauthenticated). 403 = "I know who you are but you can't do this" (unauthorized). Mixing these up is the most common REST API mistake.

questions.question: How does routing work in ASP.NET Core Web API?
questions.answer:
ASP.NET Core routing matches incoming URLs to controller actions. Two main approaches:

Attribute routing (preferred for APIs):

[ApiController]
[Route("api/[controller]")]  // api/portfolios
public class PortfoliosController : ControllerBase
{
    [HttpGet]                          // GET api/portfolios
    [HttpGet("{id:int}")]              // GET api/portfolios/42
    [HttpGet("{id}/holdings")]         // GET api/portfolios/42/holdings
    [HttpPost]                         // POST api/portfolios
    [HttpPut("{id}")]                  // PUT api/portfolios/42
    [HttpDelete("{id}")]               // DELETE api/portfolios/42
}

Route constraints ({id:int}, {id:guid}, {id:alpha}) — validate the route parameter type before matching. A request to api/portfolios/abc won't match {id:int} — returns 404 instead of a bad cast exception in the action.

Conventional routing (from app.MapControllerRoute) is typically used for MVC views, not APIs.

projectExample.explanation: FolioTrack used versioned routing via Asp.Versioning NuGet package — the version segment is part of the URL.

followUps: What are route constraints?
How do you handle route conflicts?
What is endpoint routing?
questions.tip: [ApiController] attribute changes several default behaviors: automatic 400 on model validation failure, infers [FromBody] for complex types, requires attribute routing. Always use it on API controllers.

questions.question: What is model binding in ASP.NET Core and how does it work?
questions.answer:
Model binding automatically maps HTTP request data to action method parameters. ASP.NET Core looks in these locations in order:

  • [FromRoute] — URL route segments (/api/products/{id})
  • [FromQuery] — query string (?page=1&size=20)
  • [FromBody] — request body (typically JSON). Only one [FromBody] parameter per action.
  • [FromHeader] — HTTP header
  • [FromForm] — form data (multipart/form-data for file uploads)
  • [FromServices] — resolved from DI container
// Complex type from body automatically with [ApiController]
public async Task<IActionResult> Create([FromBody] CreatePortfolioRequest request) { }

// Multiple sources in one action
public async Task<IActionResult> Update(
    [FromRoute] int id,
    [FromBody] UpdateRequest request,
    [FromQuery] bool notifyUser = false) { }

[ApiController] inference: With [ApiController], complex types default to [FromBody] and simple types default to [FromQuery] — so you often don't need explicit attributes.
followUps: What happens when model binding fails?
How do you bind a file upload?
What is custom model binding?
questions.tip: Custom model binder — useful for binding comma-separated query params to a List, or converting a string to a domain type. Implement IModelBinder and register with [ModelBinder(typeof(MyBinder))].

questions.question: What is model validation in ASP.NET Core and how do you use it?
questions.answer:
Model validation ensures incoming data meets requirements before the action body executes. Three layers:

1. Data Annotations (on the model):

public class CreateInvoiceRequest
{
    [Required]
    [StringLength(200)]
    public string ClientName { get; set; } = string.Empty;

    [Range(0.01, 1_000_000)]
    public decimal Amount { get; set; }

    [EmailAddress]
    public string? ClientEmail { get; set; }

    [RegularExpression(@"^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$")]
    public string DueDate { get; set; } = string.Empty;
}

2. Automatic 400 response with [ApiController]: If ModelState.IsValid is false, ASP.NET Core returns a 400 ProblemDetails response automatically — no if (!ModelState.IsValid) return BadRequest(...) needed.

3. FluentValidation (library) — for complex, cross-field validation logic that data annotations can't express. Supports async validation (e.g., check if email already exists in DB).
followUps: What is IValidatableObject?
FluentValidation vs data annotations — when to use which?
questions.tip: Always use FluentValidation for business rule validation ("email must be unique", "amount must be less than client's credit limit") — these need async DB calls which data annotations can't do. Use data annotations for simple format rules ([Required], [StringLength]).

questions.question: What is CORS and how do you configure it in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer:
CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a browser security mechanism that blocks a web page from making requests to a different origin (domain/port/protocol) than the one that served the page.

When FolioTrack's Angular frontend on localhost:4200 calls the API on localhost:5001, the browser sends an Origin: http://localhost:4200 header. The API must respond with Access-Control-Allow-Origin: http://localhost:4200 (or *) or the browser blocks the response.

// Program.cs
builder.Services.AddCors(options =>
{
    options.AddPolicy("FolioTrackCors", policy =>
    {
        policy.WithOrigins(
                "http://localhost:4200",   // dev
                "https://foliotrack.app")  // prod
            .AllowAnyHeader()
            .AllowAnyMethod()
            .AllowCredentials();  // required for cookies/auth
    });
});

// Middleware — BEFORE routing
app.UseCors("FolioTrackCors");
app.UseAuthentication();
app.UseAuthorization();

Preflight requests: Before non-simple requests (POST with JSON body), the browser sends an HTTP OPTIONS request to check CORS. ASP.NET Core handles this automatically when CORS middleware is configured.
followUps: What is a preflight request?
Why is CORS a browser-only restriction?
What's the difference between simple and non-simple requests?
questions.tip: CORS is enforced by the browser, not the server. A curl request or Postman call to an API with "wrong" CORS headers will succeed — only the browser enforces it. CORS is not a security mechanism for APIs; it's a browser protection for end users.

questions.question: What are action filters in ASP.NET Core Web API and how do you create a custom one?
questions.answer:
Action filters run before or after an action method executes. They implement IActionFilter (sync) or IAsyncActionFilter (async) and intercept the action pipeline.

Built-in filters: [Authorize], [ValidateAntiForgeryToken], [ResponseCache], [Produces]

Custom action filter example — execution timing:

public class ExecutionTimingFilter : IAsyncActionFilter
{
    private readonly ILogger<ExecutionTimingFilter> _logger;
    public ExecutionTimingFilter(ILogger<ExecutionTimingFilter> logger)
        => _logger = logger;

    public async Task OnActionExecutionAsync(
        ActionExecutingContext context, ActionExecutionDelegate next)
    {
        var sw = Stopwatch.StartNew();
        var result = await next();  // execute the action
        sw.Stop();

        if (sw.ElapsedMilliseconds > 500)
            _logger.LogWarning("{Action} took {ms}ms",
                context.ActionDescriptor.DisplayName, sw.ElapsedMilliseconds);
    }
}

// Register globally in Program.cs
builder.Services.AddControllers(options =>
    options.Filters.Add<ExecutionTimingFilter>());
followUps: What is the filter pipeline order?
Difference between action filter and middleware?
What is an exception filter?
questions.tip: Filter vs Middleware: Middleware operates on the raw HTTP pipeline (HttpContext). Filters operate within the MVC pipeline and have access to controller/action context (ActionDescriptor, ModelState). Use filters when you need MVC-aware logic; use middleware for HTTP-level cross-cutting concerns.

questions.question: How do you implement API versioning in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer:
API versioning lets you evolve your API without breaking existing clients. The Asp.Versioning.Mvc NuGet package (previously Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc.Versioning) handles this cleanly.

Three versioning strategies:

  • URL segment: /api/v1/portfolios — most visible, easy to test in browser, my preference.
  • Query string: /api/portfolios?api-version=1.0
  • Header: X-Api-Version: 1.0 — keeps URLs clean but harder to test
// Program.cs
builder.Services.AddApiVersioning(options =>
{
    options.DefaultApiVersion = new ApiVersion(1, 0);
    options.AssumeDefaultVersionWhenUnspecified = true;
    options.ReportApiVersions = true; // returns api-supported-versions header
}).AddMvc();

// Controller
[ApiController]
[ApiVersion("1.0")]
[ApiVersion("2.0")]
[Route("api/v{version:apiVersion}/portfolios")]
public class PortfoliosController : ControllerBase
{
    [HttpGet, MapToApiVersion("1.0")]
    public IActionResult GetV1() { /* old format */ }

    [HttpGet, MapToApiVersion("2.0")]
    public IActionResult GetV2() { /* new format */ }
}

projectExample.explanation: ServiceSelection project used URL segment versioning to add Angular Web Component-specific endpoints in v2 while maintaining the v1 REST API for backward compatibility.

followUps: How do you deprecate an API version?
How do you version Swagger/OpenAPI docs?
questions.tip: Always version from day one — retrofitting versioning is painful. Even if v1 and v2 are identical initially, the infrastructure is there. Mark deprecated versions with [Obsolete] and Deprecated = true in ApiVersionAttribute.

questions.question: How do you implement global exception handling in ASP.NET Core Web API?
questions.answer:
Never let unhandled exceptions bubble up to clients as 500 HTML error pages. Two main approaches:

1. Exception handling middleware (preferred for APIs):

// Custom middleware
app.UseExceptionHandler(errorApp =>
{
    errorApp.Run(async context =>
    {
        var feature = context.Features.Get<IExceptionHandlerFeature>()!;
        var ex = feature.Error;

        var statusCode = ex switch
        {
            KeyNotFoundException => 404,
            UnauthorizedAccessException => 403,
            ValidationException => 400,
            _ => 500
        };

        context.Response.StatusCode = statusCode;
        context.Response.ContentType = "application/problem+json";
        await context.Response.WriteAsJsonAsync(new ProblemDetails
        {
            Status = statusCode,
            Title = ex.GetType().Name,
            Detail = ex.Message  // don't expose stack trace in prod!
        });
    });
});

2. IExceptionHandler interface (.NET 8): Cleaner way — implement IExceptionHandler, register with builder.Services.AddExceptionHandler<MyHandler>(), then call app.UseExceptionHandler().
followUps: What is ProblemDetails (RFC 7807)?
How do you hide internal error details in production?
questions.tip: Never return raw exception messages or stack traces in production. Log them internally (Serilog), return a generic ProblemDetails to the client. Distinguish between 4xx (client's fault, detailed message is OK) and 5xx (server's fault, generic message only).

questions.question: What is Swagger/OpenAPI and how do you set it up in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer:
OpenAPI is a specification for describing REST APIs in machine-readable format (JSON/YAML). Swagger is the tooling ecosystem around OpenAPI — Swagger UI renders a browsable, interactive API documentation page.

In ASP.NET Core 8, Swagger support is built-in via Microsoft.AspNetCore.OpenApi. The full UI requires Swashbuckle.AspNetCore or Scalar:

// Program.cs
builder.Services.AddEndpointsApiExplorer();
builder.Services.AddSwaggerGen(options =>
{
    options.SwaggerDoc("v1", new OpenApiInfo
    {
        Title = "FolioTrack API", Version = "v1"
    });
    // Add JWT auth button in Swagger UI
    options.AddSecurityDefinition("Bearer", new OpenApiSecurityScheme
    {
        Type = SecuritySchemeType.Http,
        Scheme = "bearer",
        BearerFormat = "JWT"
    });
});

if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment())
{
    app.UseSwagger();
    app.UseSwaggerUI();
}

XML comments on controllers/actions appear in Swagger UI if you enable <GenerateDocumentationFile>true</GenerateDocumentationFile> in csproj.
followUps: What is the difference between Swashbuckle and NSwag?
How do you add Swagger for versioned APIs?
questions.tip: Scalar (newer alternative to Swagger UI) is built into .NET 9 and looks much better. If you're on .NET 8, adding Scalar takes 2 lines: builder.Services.AddOpenApi() + app.MapOpenApi(). Worth knowing.

questions.question: What is content negotiation in Web API?
questions.answer:
Content negotiation is the mechanism where the client tells the server what format it wants (via Accept header) and the server selects the best matching format from what it supports.

The client sends: Accept: application/json, application/xml;q=0.8
The server picks the best match and sets Content-Type: application/json in the response.

// ASP.NET Core: add XML formatter alongside JSON
builder.Services.AddControllers()
    .AddXmlSerializerFormatters();

// Restrict what a specific action produces
[Produces("application/json")]
[HttpGet]
public IActionResult Get() { }

[ApiController] default: ASP.NET Core returns JSON by default. If the client requests XML and you haven't added the XML formatter, it still returns JSON (best-effort match). Use [Produces] attribute to hard-code the output format of an action.
followUps: What is the Accept header?
What happens if the server doesn't support the requested format?
questions.tip: Always decorate actions with [Produces] and [Consumes] — this appears in Swagger docs and helps clients know exactly what to send/expect. [ProducesResponseType(StatusCodes.Status200OK, Type = typeof(PortfolioDto))] gives Swagger the response schema.

questions.question: How do you implement rate limiting in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer:
Rate limiting prevents API abuse by restricting how many requests a client can make in a time window. ASP.NET Core 7+ has built-in rate limiting via Microsoft.AspNetCore.RateLimiting.

// Program.cs
builder.Services.AddRateLimiter(options =>
{
    // Fixed window: 100 requests per minute per IP
    options.AddFixedWindowLimiter("fixed", config =>
    {
        config.PermitLimit = 100;
        config.Window = TimeSpan.FromMinutes(1);
        config.QueueProcessingOrder = QueueProcessingOrder.OldestFirst;
        config.QueueLimit = 5;
    });

    // Sliding window: smoother than fixed (no burst at window reset)
    options.AddSlidingWindowLimiter("api", config =>
    {
        config.PermitLimit = 100;
        config.Window = TimeSpan.FromMinutes(1);
        config.SegmentsPerWindow = 4;  // 4x15s segments
    });

    options.RejectionStatusCode = StatusCodes.Status429TooManyRequests;
});

app.UseRateLimiter();

// Apply to controller
[EnableRateLimiting("api")]
[ApiController]
public class PortfoliosController : ControllerBase { }
followUps: What is a token bucket algorithm?
How do you rate limit per user vs per IP?
What status code should rate limiting return?
questions.tip: 429 Too Many Requests is the correct status code for rate limiting. Include a Retry-After header telling the client when they can try again. For public APIs, use IP-based limits; for authenticated APIs, use user-based limits to be fair.

questions.question: What is the IActionResult return type vs specific types like Ok(), NotFound()?
questions.answer:
ASP.NET Core gives you several options for returning from an action:

1. IActionResult — most flexible:

public async Task<IActionResult> Get(int id)
{
    var item = await _service.GetAsync(id);
    if (item == null) return NotFound();     // 404
    return Ok(item);                          // 200 + JSON
    // or: return Created(url, item);         // 201
    // or: return NoContent();                // 204
}

2. ActionResult<T> — strongly typed with Swagger benefits:

// Swagger knows return type is Portfolio
public async Task<ActionResult<Portfolio>> Get(int id)
{
    var item = await _service.GetAsync(id);
    return item == null ? NotFound() : item;  // implicit conversion
}

3. Direct type return:

public async Task<Portfolio> Get(int id) { ... }

Returns 200 always — can't return 404, 400 etc. Avoid in APIs.

My preference: ActionResult<T> — gives type safety for Swagger while still allowing different status codes.
followUps: What is Results vs IActionResult in minimal APIs?
How does [ProducesResponseType] help with Swagger?
questions.tip: Use ActionResult<T> over IActionResult whenever you have a specific return type. Swagger will correctly generate the response schema. With plain IActionResult, Swagger can't infer the response type unless you add [ProducesResponseType] attributes.

questions.question: What is minimal API in ASP.NET Core and when would you use it over controllers?
questions.answer:
Minimal APIs (introduced in .NET 6) define HTTP endpoints directly in Program.cs without controllers, using lambda-based handlers:

// Minimal API
app.MapGet("/api/portfolios/{id:int}", async (int id, IPortfolioService svc) =>
{
    var portfolio = await svc.GetAsync(id);
    return portfolio == null ? Results.NotFound() : Results.Ok(portfolio);
});

app.MapPost("/api/portfolios", async (CreatePortfolioRequest req, IPortfolioService svc) =>
{
    var created = await svc.CreateAsync(req);
    return Results.Created($"/api/portfolios/{created.Id}", created);
});

When to use Minimal API:

  • Microservices with few endpoints — low ceremony, fast startup, better performance
  • Azure Functions-style compute services
  • Prototyping / simple webhooks

When to stick with controllers:

  • Complex APIs with many actions — grouping by controller is cleaner
  • Need action filters extensively (model validation, auth, caching)
  • Team familiar with MVC pattern

In the CS_PM_Service microservices project I'd use minimal APIs for the lightweight event processors but keep controllers for the complex management endpoints.
followUps: How do you add middleware/filters to minimal APIs?
How does model validation work in minimal APIs?
questions.tip: Minimal APIs and controllers can coexist in the same app. Use controllers for your complex CRUD endpoints and minimal APIs for simple webhook receivers or health check endpoints. The performance difference matters only at extreme scale.
